Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit chaotic because I'm 28 and juggling a lot more than just school. I tried to carve out time in the evenings after work and on weekends, but it wasn't always consistent. I'd usually read through materials and take handwritten notes since that's how I learn best. With 18 online courses, I had to be strategic about which ones got my attention each week. I kept a planner to track deadlines, but life often got in the way of my ideal schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and what was happening in my personal life. Some weeks I could maintain a regular evening study block, but other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to cram. Working with limited resources means I can't always control my environment or time the way traditional students might. I'd say deadlines were the biggest driver of when I actually sat down to study intensively.
Q3: Honestly, deadlines were the main thing that got me to log in. I'd also check in when I knew new materials were posted or when I needed to participate in discussions for marks. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were any announcements I'd missed. The anxiety of potentially missing something important was a real motivator, even if it wasn't the healthiest one.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became routine, usually at the start of a new module when I was feeling motivated and optimistic. I'd set reminders on my phone to check the LMS every morning. But that routine would break down when stress piled up or when I had competing responsibilities outside of school. The CBT sessions I attended actually helped me recognize these patterns and try to rebuild habits when they fell apart.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me since I'm a read-write learner. I'd download PDFs and articles so I could annotate them and study offline. The discussion forums were also helpful because interacting with peers helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. I posted quite a bit in discussions because talking through ideas with others really solidified my understanding.
Q6: I often skipped the longer video lectures or only watched portions of them because they took too much time and I couldn't always take notes effectively while watching. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant if I'd already understood the concept from reading. When resources were low on my priority list or I was pressed for time, optional materials were the first to go.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If it helped me understand something I was confused about, that made it valuable. I also appreciated activities that I could complete in chunks rather than needing long uninterrupted sessions. Given my limited resources and time constraints, efficiency was everything to me.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. Required activities got my attention because they directly impacted my grade, and with my situation, I couldn't risk failing. When I did engage with optional content, it was usually because I was genuinely curious or really struggling with a concept. There was always this guilt about not doing more optional work, but I had to be realistic about my capacity.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download readings, print what I could, and study from handwritten notes I made. I used external resources like free online articles and YouTube explanations to supplement what was in the LMS. My internet access isn't always reliable, so having offline materials was essential for me to keep up.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my printed notes or re-reading downloaded materials, and none of that showed up in the system. I'd study on the bus or during lunch breaks at work using my notes. Group study sessions with classmates happened in person too, where we'd discuss concepts without any LMS involvement. My actual study time was probably double what any log would suggest.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access materials once to download them, then study from those files repeatedly over days or weeks. The LMS would only show that single access, but I might have reviewed that PDF ten times offline. It was the most practical approach given my internet situation and the need to study during odd hours.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and engaging felt nearly impossible. When work got demanding or personal issues came up, the course became overwhelming. My stress levels were really high this term, and sometimes I'd just freeze up looking at all the tasks I needed to complete. The CBT sessions helped me work through some of those moments, but it was still a struggle.
Q13: All of those factors affected me significantly. Fatigue was huge because I was often studying late after working all day. Confusion with certain topics made me avoid those modules until the last minute. Competing responsibilities like work and family obligations meant the LMS often took a backseat. I had to constantly triage what got my attention based on urgency rather than importance.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or watch videos but my mind was somewhere else entirely. When anxiety was high, I'd be logged in but not really absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open while dealing with other stressors, technically present but mentally absent. Those sessions felt wasted, but I was trying my best to show up even when I couldn't fully engage.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments that felt overwhelming or confusing, even knowing they were important. The anxiety around potentially failing made me avoid starting rather than risk doing poorly. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was less tired, and tomorrow would become next week. It's something I've been working on in my CBT sessions, recognizing that avoidance makes the anxiety worse.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated everything about my LMS usage. I'd have bursts of intense activity right before due dates, then quieter periods in between. The deadline pressure was both motivating and anxiety-inducing. I learned to check the calendar obsessively to avoid surprises, which meant my engagement was very cyclical rather than steady.
Q17: Feedback had a big impact on my motivation. Positive feedback made me want to engage more and try harder on the next assignment. Critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me want to avoid that course for a while. I appreciated detailed feedback because it helped me understand what to improve, even when it was hard to hear.
Q18: The course structure was challenging because with 18 courses, keeping track of different formats and expectations was exhausting. Courses with clear, consistent organization were much easier to engage with. When modules were well-structured with obvious learning paths, I felt less overwhelmed. The courses that were disorganized or had confusing navigation added to my stress significantly.
Q19: Clear deadlines and organized module layouts made engagement easier. Having downloadable materials was crucial for my situation. What made it harder was when courses relied heavily on synchronous activities or assumed constant internet access. Discussion requirements actually helped me stay connected, but timed assessments were stressful given my unpredictable schedule.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying really hard to understand but struggling with the material. They might be searching through resources, rewatching videos, or reading discussions without being able to produce quality work yet. It could also be anxiety-related, where someone is engaging but too paralyzed to actually submit. Or they might be dealing with life circumstances that allow browsing but not focused work time.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. I earned a Merit despite relatively low LMS activity because so much of my real studying happened offline. My exam scores and final grade don't match what my login frequency might suggest. Students like me who download materials and study independently might look disengaged when we're actually working hard, just not in visible ways.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and participated in discussions, which is useful for tracking basic participation. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during logged sessions, and external resources used. They can't capture whether someone was genuinely learning or just clicking through. The emotional and cognitive aspects of engagement are invisible to any log.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the material and trying to connect it to what I already know. It's when I'm asking questions, taking notes, and genuinely curious about understanding deeper. For me, engagement also means showing up consistently even when it's hard, which has been a real challenge this term. It's not just about logging hours but about being mentally present and invested in learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied from printed materials or handwritten notes, which was the majority of my learning. It wouldn't capture the peer study sessions or the times I explained concepts to classmates in person. My emotional journey, the anxiety I managed, and the resilience it took to keep going despite stress, none of that appears in any data. It also wouldn't show how the CBT intervention helped me stay on track when I wanted to give up.
